title: T776 Form Canada
metaDescription: Learn how to fill out T776 form Canada step by step as a small landlord. Understand CRA deadlines, T776 line numbers, and avoid costly mistakes with our guide.
content:
As a Canadian landlord with 1-5 properties, understanding how to fill out T776 form Canada step by step is crucial for tax compliance. If you're confused or worried about this topic, you're not alone. The Canada Revenue Agency (CRA) requires landlords to report their rental income and expenses on the T776 form, which can be overwhelming. In this article, we'll guide you through the process of filling out the T776 form Canada step by step.

## Introduction to T776 Form Canada
The T776 form is used to report rental income and expenses from a rental property. The form is divided into several sections, including income, expenses, and depreciation. As a small landlord, it's essential to understand how to fill out each section accurately to avoid costly mistakes. For example, if you have a rental property in Ontario, you'll need to report your rental income on line 160 of the T1 General form, and claim your expenses on line 221.

## How to Fill Out T776 Form Canada Step by Step
To fill out the T776 form Canada step by step, start by gathering all the necessary documents, including your rental agreements, receipts for expenses, and bank statements. Then, follow these steps:
* Complete the identification section, including your name, address, and social insurance number.
* Report your rental income on line 1, including any rent received, as well as any other income from the property, such as laundry or parking fees. For example, if you received $20,000 in rent and $1,000 in laundry fees, you would report $21,000 on line 1.
* Claim your expenses on lines 5-12, including mortgage interest, property taxes, insurance, and maintenance costs. For example, if you paid $10,000 in mortgage interest and $2,000 in property taxes, you would claim $12,000 on line 5.
* Calculate your net rental income on line 22, by subtracting your total expenses from your total income. For example, if you had $21,000 in income and $12,000 in expenses, your net rental income would be $9,000.

## Understanding T776 Line Numbers
Understanding the T776 line numbers is crucial for accurate reporting. For example, line 5 is used to report mortgage interest, while line 8 is used to report property taxes. Line 12 is used to report miscellaneous expenses, such as accounting fees or property management fees. As a small landlord, it's essential to keep accurate records of your expenses to ensure you're claiming everything you're eligible for. You can learn more about [CRA-Compliant Bookkeeping for Canadian Landlords: The Complete Guide](https://www.rentalops.ca/blog/cra-compliant-bookkeeping-canadian-landlords-complete-guide) to help you stay organized.

## CRA Deadlines and Penalties
The CRA deadline for filing the T776 form is April 30th of each year, unless you or your spouse/common-law partner are self-employed, in which case the deadline is June 15th. Failure to file on time can result in penalties, including a late-filing penalty of 5% of the balance owing, plus 1% per month, up to a maximum of 12 months. For example, if you owe $10,000 in taxes and file 6 months late, you could be charged a penalty of $1,300. Don't make the mistake of [Failing to Report Rental Income to CRA: A Costly Mistake for Canadian Landlords](https://www.rentalops.ca/blog/failing-to-report-rental-income-to-cra-a-costly-mistake-for-canadian-landlords), and understand the importance of reporting your rental income accurately.

For example, if you spend 2-3 hours per month tracking receipts and expenses manually, that's equivalent to $100-$150 per month, or $1,200-$1,800 per year, assuming a $50 per hour wage. ## Common Mistakes
As a small landlord, it's easy to make mistakes when filling out the T776 form Canada step by step. Here are three common mistakes to avoid:
* Failing to keep accurate records of expenses, resulting in missed deductions.
* Incorrectly claiming depreciation on assets, resulting in an audit.
* Failing to report all rental income, resulting in penalties and interest. You can learn more about [Navigating the CRA Principal Residence Exemption: A Guide for Canadian Landlords](https://www.rentalops.ca/blog/navigating-the-cra-principal-residence-exemption-a-guide-for-canadian-landlords) to help you understand the rules around principal residence exemption.

## Key Takeaways
Here are the key takeaways for filling out the T776 form Canada step by step:
* Keep accurate records of income and expenses.
* Understand the T776 line numbers and claim all eligible deductions.
* File on time to avoid penalties and interest.
* Consider using a comprehensive solution like RentalOps to save time and money.
* Stay informed about CRA rules and regulations, such as the [Rental Income Tax in Canada: Complete Guide for Small Landlords (2026)](https://www.rentalops.ca/blog/rental-income-tax-in-canada-complete-guide-for-small-landlords-2026).

## Conclusion
Filling out the T776 form Canada step by step can be a complex and time-consuming process, but with the right tools and knowledge, you can ensure you're taking advantage of all the deductions you're eligible for.
